content.problemStatement:
Upcasting is a type of operation in Java where we assign an object of a derived class to a reference variable of its base class. Upcasting is safe and doesn't require an explicit cast because the derived class object inherits all the properties and behaviors of the base class.

In this example, we have a base class `Animal` and a derived class `Dog`. We create an instance of the `Dog` class and assign it to a reference variable of type `Animal` (upcasting). Even though the reference variable `animal` is of type `Animal`, it points to a `Dog` object. When we call the `makeSound` method on the `animal` reference, it calls the `Dog` class's implementation of the method because the actual object is of the `Dog` class.

Upcasting is commonly used in Java, especially in scenarios where you want to work with a collection of objects that are of different derived classes but share a common base class or interface. It allows you to treat objects in a more general way, making your code more flexible and extensible.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
